story:
Before I explain what I did and why, you need to know this: Harold was no prize. He retired early from John Deere not because of a boss, but because he couldn’t stand the guy he trained who stood next to him on the line—night shift, a little teasing, and Harold folded. He’s a bit overweight, balding, and touchy about both. At home he lived in his recliner, TV blaring until six, pausing only long enough to eat off a tray. I paid the bills. He assumed nothing would change; I was finished.

I took my own life. I won’t give you the how—there’s no point—but I will tell you I ended up somewhere else, and it wasn’t what I expected. No thunder, no answers to the big questions. Nobody knows who shot Kennedy, where Bigfoot hides, or whether Ancient Aliens ever got anything right. Oddly enough, I felt no regret, no guilt, no lightning bolts. Time and space bend here, which sounds grand until you’re stuck waiting and no clock ticks the way you want it to.

The real kicker was the paperwork, so to speak. Turns out that “until death do us part” is not the loophole I thought. Because it wasn’t technically my time, I’d jumped the line and broken a contract. Suicide isn’t the scandal over here; ditching your responsibilities out of order is. The rulekeepers told me I still owed Harold the care I’d promised until he died. My options: prove he drove me to it, show a divorce in progress, or find him a new wife to love, cherish, and obey—yes, they said that—until his actual death. Or I could wait for him to arrive and settle it then. “Time is relative,” they said, like that helped, and scattered.

As if to salt the wound, Harold’s mother is here. Same sour expression, same judgment. First thing out of her mouth: “Where’s Harold?” When I said, “Alive and probably asleep in his recliner,” she sniffed, “Figures. I told him not to marry you,” then wandered off. Not helpful. Not surprising.

I argued, loudly, for common sense. Surely there were exceptions to this idiotic rule? The welcome group smiled, reminded me about relativity, and disappeared. Fine. I couldn’t find Harold a wife when I was alive with a phone and a calendar; how was I supposed to do it dead? So I went to check on him.

He was right where I expected: recliner, TV glowing, a wooden TV tray now instead of the old metal one. Progress, I guess. “Harold,” I whispered. He jerked up, eyes wide. “What are you doing here?” Then, louder: “Get out of my house.” I was about to explain the cosmic fine print when someone brushed straight through me, cold as a wire, arm extended, gun raised. The shot cracked. I snapped back to my side. A breath later, Harold stood next to me, blinking.

“What happened?” he asked.

“You got shot,” I said. “Who was that?”

“I think it was Gloria’s husband,” he muttered.

“Gloria? Across the street, Gloria?”

“The same. It’s a long story.” He squinted at me. “Aren’t you dead?”

“It’s relative, Harold,” I said. “It’s all relative.”
